channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Tony Gonzales’s Scandal Forces Him Into Runoff Election in South Texas
item.description: Representative Gonzales did not get 50 percent or more from voters in the Republican primary race in Texas’ 23rd Congressional District, as required by law to win the nomination. He has battled allegations that he had an inappropriate relationship with a former staff member.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales at a House Committee on Homeland Security hearing in Washington, D.C., last month.

item.title: Dan Crenshaw Loses to Steve Toth for Texas District 2
item.description: The victory by Steve Toth, a hard-line Texas state representative, underscored how even a conservative House member could lose Republican voters by breaking with President Trump.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Dan Crenshaw, Republican of Texas, faced a primary challenge from Steve Toth, one of the most conservative members of the Texas House.

item.title: Republican Primary for N.C. State Senate Seat Remains Too Close to Call
item.description: But that did not stop Sam Page, a small-town sheriff steeped in President Trump’s political movement, from declaring victory in the race.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina

item.title: Progressives sweep three conservative Democrats from the North Carolina statehouse
item.description: Tired of Democrats who were siding with Republicans in the legislature, liberal groups took aim at three in the primaries and beat each of them.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: Travis Dove for The New York Times
item.media:description: Campaign signs outside an early voting site in Charlotte, N.C., last month.

item.title: Court Rulings Throw Dallas County Vote Tally Into Uncertainty
item.description: Hours after a district judge ordered the county to keep its polling places open, the Texas Supreme Court temporarily blocked the decision.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning, Nick Corasaniti and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Emil T. Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: A polling site at Samuell Grand Recreation Center in Dallas, on Tuesday.

item.title: Bobby Pulido Wins Democratic Primary in Texas’ 15th District
item.description: Mr. Pulido, a centrist and Latin Grammy Award-winning Tejano singer, defeated Ada Cuellar, an emergency room doctor who challenged his center-left views on abortion and connections to the Democratic establishment.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bobby Pulido will face Representative Monica de la Cruz for Texas’s 15th Congressional District, after winning Tuesday’s Democratic primary.

item.title: Cornyn and Paxton Head to Runoff in Bitter Texas Senate Primary
item.description: Neither Senator John Cornyn nor Attorney General Ken Paxton secured a majority on Tuesday, setting up a May runoff after a bitter and costly Republican primary.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Neither Senator John Cornyn, Republican of Texas, left, nor Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, secured a majority of votes on Tuesday in their contest for the Republican nomination for Mr. Cornyn’s Senate seat.

item.title: There are a few key counties to watch in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang
item.media:credit: Ilana Panich-Linsman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Candidates’ signs in Austin, Texas, on Tuesday.

item.title: The Texas Senate primaries set a record for ad spending.
item.dc:creator: Matt Zdun
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: About $70 million was spent on ads favoring Senator John Cornyn.

item.title: U.S. Takes Military Action in Ecuador Against ‘Terrorist Organizations’
item.description: U.S. Special Forces soldiers are advising and supporting Ecuadorian commandos on raids across the country against suspected drug shipment facilities and other drug-related sites.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Marcos Pin/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Ecuadorian Navy stand guard in the facilities of the Coast Guard Command at the Port of Manta, Ecuador, in 2025.

item.title: Congress Splits Over Iran War as Senate Faces a Vote
item.description: The administration’s shifting justifications for the military operation alarmed Democrats, who said no clear rationale had been given. Republicans struggled to echo the evolving explanations.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio spoke to reporters on Tuesday about the conflict with Iran.

item.title: Roy Cooper and Michael Whatley Advance in North Carolina Senate Race
item.description: North Carolina has broken national Democrats’ hearts for nearly two decades, but this time they hope that Roy Cooper, the former governor, can once again find a path to victory.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times; Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Roy Cooper, left, the former Democratic governor of North Carolina, and Michael Whatley, the former chairman of the Republican National Committee.

item.title: Mapping Damage to Iran’s Ballistic Missile Sites With Satellite Images
item.description: A New York Times analysis finds evidence of strikes on Iran’s underground ballistic missile sites and facilities targeted last June.
item.dc:creator: Samuel Granados, Daniel Wood and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: A building at a missile facility near Isfahan appears to be severely damaged.

item.title: Is This the Year Texas Goes Blue?
item.description: The signs are familiar: an unpopular Republican president, well-funded Democrats and anecdotes of disaffected G.O.P. and independent voters.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

item.title: Surge of First-Time Democratic Primary Voters Adds Uncertainty to Texas Races
item.description: A surge of voters who had previously participated only in general elections turned out in the Democratic primary, a shift that could make key Texas races harder to predict.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters waited in line to cast their ballots at E.M. Daggett Elementary School in Fort Worth on Tuesday.

item.title: The House Races to Watch in Tuesday’s Primary Elections
item.description: Democrats are playing defense on a redrawn House map in Texas, while incumbents in both parties face fierce primary challenges in Texas and North Carolina.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Al Green, a Democrat in Houston, is in danger of losing his redrawn seat in the 18th Congressional District.

item.title: Judge Extends Voting in Dallas County After Complaints on Confusion
item.description: The judge extended voting until 9 p.m. Democrats had argued that a rule change spurred by Republicans confused voters who went to the wrong polling places.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Emil T. Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jasmine Crockett speaking to reporters outside Oak Lawn Library in Dallas on Tuesday.

item.title: Police Arrest a Masked Man Outside Ken Paxton Watch Party
item.description: Officers found ammunition when searching his car after reports of a suspicious person. He was arrested on traffic charges, the Dallas police said.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers recovered magazines of ammunition from a car apparently driven by the man taken into custody.

item.title: What to Know About Jasmine Crockett, the Texas Democratic Senate Candidate
item.description: The congresswoman, who has established herself as a firebrand among fellow Democratic leaders, is looking to get on the ballot for Senate in November.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jasmine Crockett of Dallas is running in the Democratic primary for Senate.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About James Talarico, Democratic Senate Hopeful in Texas
item.description: A Presbyterian seminarian, the Democrat has put his faith at the forefront of his political pitch.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico sprinkles biblical references into his political arguments.

item.title: Trump Tries to Quiet Claims Among Supporters That Israel Dragged Him Into War
item.description: Many of President Trump’s allies have urged him and his Make America Great Again movement to shift away from their close ties to Israel and military entanglements in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump attempted to extinguish the idea that Israel forced the United States to enter a war with Iran.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Senate Candidate Ken Paxton of Texas
item.description: The Texas attorney general rode into office on a Tea Party wave and has faced several high-profile scandals during his tenure.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton has aligned himself with President Trump.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Senator John Cornyn of Texas
item.description: A longtime fixture of Republican politics in Texas, he has occasionally criticized President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn speaking at a rally in Houston last month.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Representative Wesley Hunt of Texas
item.description: He entered the Republican Senate primary late and has been an underdog in the contest.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wesley Hunt has said he is running to offer alternative to John Cornyn and Ken Paxton.

item.title: U.S. Releases Names of Soldiers Killed in War With Iran
item.description: Six U.S. troops were killed by an Iranian drone that exploded at a military base in Kuwait.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon released the names of four U.S. troops who were killed by an Iranian drone that detonated in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait on Sunday,

item.title: Who Has Trump Endorsed in the Texas Primary Elections?
item.description: President Trump is backing numerous candidates running for re-election but has stayed quiet on a few key races.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Monday.

item.title: Hot Air Balloon Rescue in Texas After It Strikes a Cell Tower
item.description: Two people were left dangling from a communications tower near Longview, Texas, on Saturday until more than a dozen firefighters could scale the equivalent of a skyscraper to reach them.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: Resurfaced Affair Allegations Intensify Gonzales’s Primary Challenge
item.description: Representative Tony Gonzales, a Republican, is fighting to hold onto his job as he faces a primary challenge from the right and fresh allegations of personal indiscretions that have rocked his campaign.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales, Republican of Texas, at the Capitol earlier this month.

item.title: ‘Cheap’ and ‘Appalling’: Trump’s Ballroom Plans Receive a Flood of Negative Comments
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission received about 32,000 messages during its public comment period. Suffice it to say: Many people are not happy with the president’s ballroom plans.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s demolition of the East Wing and remaking of the White House in his own style have struck a nerve with Americans.

item.title: Texas State House Race Features Candidate Pardoned for Jan. 6 by Trump
item.description: A conservative member of the Texas Legislature is facing a tough challenge from an Air Force veteran who went to prison for breaching the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy and J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Larry Brock, who spent two years in prison for entering the Capitol during the Jan. 6 attack, is challenging State Representative Jared Patterson for his seat in the Texas Legislature.

item.title: Lutnick Agrees to Testify in House Epstein Investigation
item.description: The Commerce Secretary, who misrepresented his relationship with the convicted sex offender, has volunteered to appear, the House Oversight Committee’s leader said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Yuri Gripas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Howard Lutnick, commerce secretary, testifying before the Senate earlier this month.

item.title: A Costly, Bitter Senate Primary Splits Texas Republicans
item.description: The bruising contest between Senator John Cornyn and Attorney General Ken Paxton reflects a broader struggle between establishment Republicans and the party’s ascendant right flank.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Texas attorney general, Ken Paxton, is a political survivor, hoping to defeat Senator John Cornyn in Tuesday’s Republican primary.

item.title: Trump Lays Out His ‘Worst Case’ Scenario in Iran
item.description: Who will lead Iran once the war is over? “Most of the people we had in mind are dead,” says President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A black flag flying over Tehran on Monday after Ayatollah Khamenei was killed in U.S.-Israeli strikes.

item.title: How Iran Is Using Drones to Retaliate Against the U.S. and Allies
item.description: The Pentagon and Middle Eastern countries say that most of the drones have been intercepted. But some have slipped through and caused damage.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Helene Cooper and Sheera Frenkel
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Hamad I Mohammed/Reuters
item.media:description: Smoke rising from a building that was hit in an Iranian drone strike in Manama, Bahrain, on Saturday.

item.title: Texas Republican Primaries Test Voters’ Appetite for MAGA Candidates Beyond the Senate Race
item.description: Texas voters are deciding high-stakes Republican primaries for attorney general, comptroller and other statewide offices, testing MAGA influence and Gov. Greg Abbott’s endorsement power.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Republican primaries in Texas could signal just how much further to the right voters want to push the state.

item.title: Austin Bar Shooting Victim Jorge Pederson Honored
item.description: Family and friends on Tuesday paid tribute to Jorge Pederson, 30, and the two others who were killed in the attack outside a bar in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:credit: Nuri Vallbona/Reuters
item.media:description: Three people were killed in a shooting at Buford’s bar in Austin over the weekend.

item.title: ‘Beyond the pale’: More than $10 million has been spent in one N.C. primary.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Berger, right, is running for re-election to the State Senate in a race that has drawn an inordinate amount of spending.

item.title: Trump Immigration Sweeps Spawn Underground Safe Houses
item.description: In Springfield, Ohio, some Americans have converted their basements and spare bedrooms into shelters for immigrant families who could be targeted in raids.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Maddie McGarvey
item.media:description: A spare bedroom in a Springfield, Ohio, house prepared for Haitians, in case the immigrants didn’t feel safe in their own homes.

item.title: As Trump Bashes Spain on Military Spending, German Chancellor Merz Looks On
item.description: Friedrich Merz has called for greater European unity, but he did not publicly object to the president rebuking Spain and Britain during an Oval Office meeting.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Friedrich Merz, the chancellor of Germany, in the Oval Office on Tuesday. Mr. Merz did not object as Mr. Trump rebuked European allies.

item.title: I.M.F. Warns Iran War Could Hurt Global Economy
item.description: Inflation fears over higher energy prices could force central banks to take action, a top fund official said.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The war with Iran could take a toll on the global economy, the International Monetary Fund said on Tuesday.

item.title: In the Texas Senate Primary Election, James Talarico is Trying a Message of Love
item.description: James Talarico, a Democratic candidate for the U.S. Senate, is embracing a compassionate campaign approach in a bare-knuckled political environment.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Representative James Talarico during a campaign event at the University of Houston on Monday. Mr. Talarico is facing Representative Jasmine Crockett in the Democratic Senate primary on Tuesday.

item.title: You Break It, You Own It? Not for Trump When It Comes to Iran.
item.description: “We’ll see what happens with the people,” President Trump said as he appeared to be distancing himself from the longer-term consequences of the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Iranian flag on Tuesday at the site of a diplomatic police station in Tehran that was targeted by U.S.-Israeli airstrikes.

item.title: DHS Investigates Gregory Bovino’s Remarks About Prosecutor’s Jewish Faith
item.description: Mr. Bovino, who was the face of the Trump administration’s immigration crackdowns in American cities, was reported to have made disparaging comments in reference to the U.S. attorney in Minnesota, who is an Orthodox Jew.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gregory Bovino, a senior Border Patrol commander, during Operation Midway Blitz in Chicago last fall.

item.title: Apalachee High School Shooting: Colin Gray Found Guilty of Murder
item.description: A jury found that Colin Gray, the father of the suspect, bore criminal responsibility for the attack at a Georgia school by failing to heed warnings of his son’s struggles.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Johnny Kauffman
item.media:credit: Abbey Cutrer/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Colin Gray, the father of the suspect in the Apalachee High School shooting, during his trial in Winder, Ga.

item.title: Trump Administration, in Reversal, Tries to Continue Fight Against Law Firms
item.description: The administration told a court on Monday that it was abandoning its defense of executive orders targeting the firms. But on Tuesday, the Justice Department abruptly changed its position.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt, Jonah E. Bromwich and Devlin Barrett
item.category: Wilmer Cutler Pickering Hale and Dorr LLP
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department indicated it would continue defending the orders in an email to the law firms on Tuesday morning.
